question: What is an ANR? How do you avoid them?
answer:
ANR = Application Not Responding. The system shows the dialog when:
• The main thread has not handled the next input event within ~5 seconds.
• A BroadcastReceiver hasn't returned within ~10s (foreground) / ~60s (background).
• A foreground service hasn't started within timeout.

Avoid by NEVER blocking the main thread:
• Move I/O / DB / heavy CPU off main using coroutines + Dispatchers.IO / Default.
• Don't call Thread.sleep on main.
• Watch out for synchronous IPC (`Binder.transact`).
• Profile main-thread work with the Android Studio Profiler → CPU → System Trace.
• Use `StrictMode` in debug builds — fails fast on disk/network on main.

Monitor real-user ANRs through Play Console → Vitals or Firebase Crashlytics.

question: How do you find and fix memory leaks on Android?
answer:
Common Android leaks:
• Long-lived references holding Activity/Fragment after destroy (callbacks, static fields).
• Inner classes / lambdas capturing `this` (Activity, Fragment).
• Listeners registered with system services that aren't unregistered.
• Singletons holding Context (use `applicationContext` not Activity).
• Bitmaps not recycled; large drawables in static fields.
• Coroutines launched in GlobalScope without cancellation.

Detect:
• LeakCanary — drop-in library that watches Activity/Fragment lifecycles, detects retained instances, prints a leak trace.
• Android Studio Memory Profiler → Capture heap → look for retained Activity instances.
• `dumpsys meminfo <package>` from adb.

Fix:
• Use `viewLifecycleOwner` in Fragments for view-bound state.
• Pass `applicationContext` to long-lived helpers.
• Cancel coroutines (`viewModelScope` does it for you).
• Unregister listeners in onPause/onStop or use `DisposableEffect`.

question: What is jank? How do you measure and fix dropped frames?
answer:
Jank = visible stutter caused by missed frame deadlines (every 16.7ms at 60Hz, 8.3ms at 120Hz). Caused by main thread or GPU work overrunning the budget.

Measure:
• Layout Inspector / GPU rendering profile (developer options).
• Android Studio Profiler → CPU → System Trace → look for frame markers > 16ms.
• Macrobenchmark with `JankStats` library — produces histograms of frame durations on real users.
• `dumpsys gfxinfo <package> framestats` — frame timeline.

Common fixes:
• Reduce overdraw — flatten view hierarchies, remove unnecessary backgrounds.
• Defer work — image decoding, JSON parsing off-main.
• Use Lazy* in Compose for long lists; don't render the whole list eagerly.
• Use `RecyclerView` view types + DiffUtil; pre-fetch items.
• Use baseline profiles (see separate question).
• Cache layouts that don't change.

question: What is R8 / ProGuard? What does it do for your app?
answer:
R8 is the Android default code shrinker / optimizer / obfuscator that runs in release builds (replaces ProGuard since AGP 3.4).

It does:
• Tree shaking — drops unused classes/methods/fields.
• Optimization — inlining, dead-code elimination.
• Obfuscation — renames classes to short names like `a.b.c`.
• Resource shrinking — drops unused drawables/strings (with `shrinkResources true`).

Results: smaller APK, marginal performance improvement, partial defense against trivial reverse-engineering (real attackers will still de-obfuscate).

Gotchas:
• Reflection breaks if R8 renames classes — keep with `-keep` rules. `proguard-rules.pro` in your module.
• Many libraries ship their own consumer rules; you usually don't need to add anything.
• If a release build crashes only after R8, the reason is almost always `-keep` rules.
• Always test the release variant before shipping; don't trust debug-only behavior.

question: What are baseline profiles and what do they buy you?
answer:
Baseline profiles are AOT-compilation hints shipped with your app. The Android runtime (ART) precompiles the listed methods on first install / app update so they run fast immediately, instead of being JIT-compiled lazily.

Results documented by Google on real apps:
• ~30% faster cold startup.
• ~20% faster first scroll.
• Smoother early frames on key user journeys.

Workflow:
• Use the `androidx.benchmark:benchmark-macro-junit4` library to author a `BaselineProfileGenerator` test that walks through critical user journeys (launch, scroll, tap a key tab).
• `./gradlew :app:generateBaselineProfile` runs it on a test device; outputs `baseline-prof.txt` consumed by AGP.
• AGP packages the profile in the APK / AAB; ART picks it up.
• Combine with the Macrobenchmark module to verify gains.

The Jetpack Compose toolkit ships an opinionated baseline profile by default; custom profiles add app-specific code paths.

question: How do you optimize app startup time on Android?
answer:
Startup is split into:
• Cold — process freshly created, Application + first Activity created.
• Warm — process exists, Activity recreated.
• Hot — Activity already in memory; just brought to front.

Measure:
• Logcat `Displayed com.app/.MainActivity: +XXX ms` — visual completion.
• `adb shell am start -W` — system reports startup time.
• Macrobenchmark with `StartupTimingMetric`.
• Firebase Performance / Play Vitals — real-user data.

Optimizations:
• Defer SDK initialisation (analytics, image cache, work scheduling) to AFTER the first frame using `App Startup` library or `Lazy<T>`.
• Avoid heavy work in `Application.onCreate`. Keep it < 200ms.
• Inflate splash with `androidx.core.splashscreen` — system handles it consistently.
• Use baseline profiles.
• Remove unused content providers and broadcast receivers; manifest scanning isn't free.
• Lazy-load Activity views (use ConstraintLayout to keep deep hierarchies flat).
